segments.title: YouTube historian as public history practice
segments.summary: A channel-advice episode frames online history as copyright care, source hunting, research humility, audience management, and myth checking.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains why his channel avoids risky archival footage, why books and translated sources still matter, how live questions require humility and correction, and how public-facing historians need to test myths against dates and evidence.

evidence.note: The episode introduces copyright and footage-claim risk as a reason for the channel's restrained presentation style.
The research section emphasizes books, translation, wide-ranging naval history, and using research skill rather than assuming omniscience.
The live-stream section covers knowing when to decline an answer, accepting correction, contemporary-event caution, and moderated chat.
The myth-handling section tests a conspiracy claim against dates, institutional facts, and historical plausibility.
